chapters.title: Real productivity is about missing out on trivial things
chapters.text:
Today, most of us struggle to keep our heads above the proverbial waters no matter who we are or what we do for a living. We are highly overwhelmed by everything, from our lives to our responsibilities and even by our to-do lists.

At this fast pace, productivity becomes extremely important, and we start associating efficiency with taking on more tasks. Instead, it’s about making the conscious choice to reduce the unnecessary noise in our lives and focus on the things that matter to us.

Productivity is about finding happiness in a life centered on our priorities.

Most people need to be made aware of the fact that being busy is different from being productive. They believe they must always be engaged and have their days filled with activities to be happy. Unfortunately, they become exhausted and frustrated after spending months loaded with tasks. Despite keeping themselves busy, they remain unsatisfied, unable to reach their much-desired success.

Productivity is not about doing more, it’s doing what’s most important. ~ Tanya Dalton

It’s time we debunked the myth that being busy equals being productive. After all, nothing could be further from the truth. Let’s stop mindlessly glorifying this hustle mindset and redefine what it means to be both happy and efficient. When we do that, we can finally live the fulfilled and healthy lives we want.

Dive into this summary to learn how to build a life based on your priorities.

chapters.title: Learn to let go
chapters.text:
Most of us desire acknowledgment for our hard work and dedication. That’s why we wake up daily and strive to accomplish as much as possible. Unfortunately, many of us associate our value with how much we do. We consider stress as a badge of honor that proves our worth.

Society has even convinced us that we’re unsuccessful if we’re not busy. That’s why we try to achieve balance by doing everything at once; that’s when we start running between work, personal life, and hobbies. But investing our time in everything at once doesn’t lead to more success. The reason is that balance is nothing more than a mirage. It is practically impossible for anyone to divide their attention equally.

Magic doesn’t happen when life is centered and balanced — it happens when we lean into our priorities. ~ Tanya Dalton

We usually have three areas in our lives that require attention: work, home, and personal. While each holds value, it’s not always possible to keep them balanced simultaneously. We can’t divide our time, energy, and concentration equally among them — it takes immense energy to keep the buckets filled equally.

We sometimes have to move away from balance to achieve our desired lives.

We must prioritize and give our most valuable resources — time, energy, and concentration — to what’s most important. Allocating time to our top priorities means sacrificing other tasks on our to-do list, as we cannot devote equal time to everything.

In reality, striving for perfect balance in all areas of our lives can be counterproductive. Unfortunately, this prevents us from directing our resources toward our desired destination. A passionate dedication to preserving balance can lead to a busy but meaningless life.

To live a happy, meaningful, and content life, we must risk and sacrifice this balance. It’s okay to focus more on work for certain periods of our life and then pay more attention to personal life later. We have to let go of the notion of “doing everything at once.”

chapters.title: Follow your North Star
chapters.text:
Throughout history, the North Star has been an essential guiding point in the night sky. Long before the invention of maps, seafarers, and adventurers relied on this crucial tool to navigate their way to their desired destination.

Although we are not seafarers, we still need a trustworthy guide to navigating life’s challenges. In truth, we do not accidentally discover a satisfying and successful path in life. Instead, this path is something we must actively construct and choose for ourselves. Our North Star is also a guide to identify and use to direct our professional journeys.

If you fail to establish your North Star, someone else will select it for you. In that case, you’ll act chaotically instead of concentrating on progressing in the direction that truly matters to you. Using this direction properly will show you how to use your time wisely and encourage better choices.

How do you find your guiding Star?

Your North Star is composed of three major elements:

• Mission statement
• Vision statement
• Core values

Each of these components provides insight into your true identity.

“What do I do?” is crucial to your mission statement. Your response should be genuine to yourself and clear enough for others to understand your actions and the rationale behind them. Ensure it’s concise; your mission statement should be easy to memorize and repeat.

Your vision statement addresses the question, “Where am I heading?” It signifies where you envision yourself in life, revealing where you want to be in the future rather than where you are now. It establishes your course and identifies your desired endpoint.

You will define your core values by considering how to act upon both statements. These fundamental principles steer your actions in the right direction and influence your choices. They must be easy to work upon, allowing for effortless integration into your everyday routine.

To stay clear on your success path, imagine your future desired self and define how to get there.

Incorporating your mission, vision, and core values into your life will prevent you from feeling overwhelmed. The North Star will create the necessary direction, shedding light on which steps to take and how to initiate them.

chapters.title: Goal-setting is more important than you think
chapters.text:
Many individuals are unaware that productivity is not about exerting significant effort but concentrating on a particular goal. Imagine you’re directing a magnifying glass to start a fire. One method of achieving this is by establishing clear goals.

Your goals can become that magnifying glass that directs your energy and hones your focus. They aid in filtering information and identifying the necessary steps to advance toward your North Star.

Harvard research backs this idea. A major study involving MBA students has shown that establishing clear, written goals and devising plans to achieve them is imperative for success later in life. Among those surveyed, 84% had no goals, 13% had goals but didn’t write them down, and only 3% had written goals with specific plans. After ten years, the students who had set vague goals earned twice as much as those who had set no goals, while the 3% who had written goals made ten times the combined amount of the remaining 97%.

As this study shows, setting goals is effective as it helps us clarify what we want to accomplish. Goal-setting simplifies our journey and describes where we need to focus our energy.

You must set goals to determine the direction of your success journey.

At the same time, we must learn to say “no” to things that don’t matter as much, which means uncommitting from goals and tasks of unequal importance. The word “uncommit” can be very uncomfortable, as we often assume that saying no to others means we are letting them down.

The truth is, we’re already saying no all the time. When you agree to something, you’re also declining something else. Prioritizing other people’s needs and goals takes away from your own.

To prioritize the goals that matter, it’s not enough to just say no to other commitments. You need to actively commit to the things that are most important to you. You can focus your efforts and make a real impact by scheduling time and energy for these causes, people, and tasks.

Without clear goals, you’ll be easily distracted by everything else. So take the time to identify what truly matters to you and let go of anything that doesn’t align with those values.

chapters.title: True productivity lies in working toward being effective
chapters.text:
You’ve probably come across clickbait articles promising you can churn out a year’s content for your blog in three days or fooling you into believing that a week is more than enough to write a book.

In our effort to be efficient, we often send ourselves into a detrimental frenzy, moving as quickly as possible from one task to the next. We believe that the quality of our day is determined by how many tasks we can check off our to-do list. So, we rush and hurry mindlessly around all day without taking a moment to really live.

We often feel exhausted and as though we don’t accomplish enough, even when we skip meals and multitask. Instead of striving for efficiency and pushing ourselves to the brink every day, we must pause and ask ourselves why we are doing these tasks in the first place.

Do they actually need to be done? Do we have to try to do everything at once? Being productive is not about checking things off our to-do list. Instead, it points us toward being more mindful and intentional with our time.

To truly achieve productivity, it’s essential to not just focus on being efficient but also on being effective. It means questioning whether a task is necessary and aligns with your goals before diving into it. Efficiency alone may not lead to the desired outcome if the task itself is not effective or relevant.

To be efficient means completing numerous tasks, while being effective involves focusing on the crucial work.

Prioritizing quantity over quality is never a wise decision. Our fixation on meeting deadlines causes us to overlook the harmful effects of our supposed “efficient” processes, such as multitasking, skipping meals, and neglecting rest, which can impede our progress. It’s time we realize that focusing on being efficient only wastes our time and does nothing to improve our productivity.

Did you know? You have control over your time, and it’s not the reverse. To use this control, you must realize and acknowledge that you don’t have to do more. Instead, you must change your mindset about being busy and focus on real productivity by investing your time in the most important things.

chapters.title: Dedicate the greater portion of your time to the essentials
chapters.text:
Your effectiveness is mainly determined by how you handle your priorities. Focusing on what’s important is critical, rather than trying to juggle everything at once and feeling overwhelmed. You can improve your productivity by using the Pareto or 80/20 principles. Vilfredo Pareto first spotted the 80/20 ratio occurring in nature before introducing it into the mainstream in the 1700s. He observed similar distributions in the physical world and created a formula.

The 80/20 principle explains that 20% of our efforts lead to 80% of our results. This principle still perfectly applies to our lives today:

• ​​20% of a call gives 80% of the information.
• We wear 20% of our wardrobe 80% of the time.
• 20% of customers account for 80% of sales.
• 20% of our team completes 80% of our work.

These examples demonstrate how often there is an imbalance between the effort we invest and the outcomes we achieve. The Pareto Principle has been proven repeatedly in all areas of life, showing that not all things are equal and we shouldn’t treat them as such. Think of how this principle can be relevant in your own life — do you notice this imbalance?

You can achieve more when you narrow down your focus.

According to the Pareto Principle, most of your success will come from only 20% of your actions. Examining and pinpointing those essential tasks and devoting most of our attention to them is crucial. Concentrate on what brings you the most results instead of simultaneously dividing your attention among many tasks.

Give it a shot: concentrate on the top 20% of your clients for a quarter and observe if it boosts your sales. Naturally, it’s essential not to neglect the remaining 80%, but try directing most of your focus to the top 20% in this experiment.

Also, don’t let insignificant things take over your day. Limit the time you give to trivial tasks so you can focus on the essentials.

chapters.title: Use the CLEAR framework to determine priorities
chapters.text:
Before you can effectively channel your resources — focus, time, and energy — toward what matters, you must first understand what is essential.

When you have many tasks to handle, it can be tough to determine which ones are essential, leading to priority blindness. It’s like carrying a heavy load that keeps you from achieving your desired life. To overcome this blindness, ask yourself, “What can I let go of to stay afloat?” This exercise helps to identify the most crucial tasks that need your attention, even when it seems overwhelming.

However, it’s not always easy to choose what’s important. To simplify this, Tonya Dalton developed the CLEAR framework, which separates urgent tasks from important ones:

• C — connected to your North Star
• L — linked to a goal
• E — essential
• A — advantageous
• R — reality-based

To determine whether a task is critical, ask yourself if it’s connected to your overall goals or “North Star.” Your guiding principle, North Star, determines your priorities and where you direct your energy. So, make sure any task that you tag as important is:

• Tied to your mission statement
• Aligned with your core values
• Moving you closer to your vision

After this, analyze if this activity connects to your goal. Your goals play a crucial role in defining the order of your priority list in everyday life.

Your daily tasks should reflect and align with your goals – this is the best way to ensure you take small steps on the longer path to success daily. These gradual steps help build momentum, culminating in more substantial progress toward your aims.

Is the task essential? Is it something that needs to be done by you? Or can this task be delegated to someone else?

Does it have an advantage? You must ensure you’re working on tasks with a great return on investment. The time you put into your daily activities must pay off later in your success journey.

It’s wise to regularly question if the way you’re investing your time aligns with your priorities.

Finally, is the task reality-based? Sometimes, we assume certain tasks are essential because others expect us to do them, even if we don’t necessarily want to. So, ensure you are not doing that task to meet other people’s expectations or to feel good about yourself.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
If you spend your days overwhelmed and pushed to your limit, it is not because you have too much to do but because you do not know where to start. Most people find it very difficult to figure out where to start because they are too busy saying yes to every opportunity that comes their way.

As Tanya Dalton makes us understand, if you want to wrap up your days feeling fulfilled, you have to stop focusing on other people’s priorities and trying to keep up with everyone else.

Busyness is not the same thing as productivity. In fact, doing less is the key to productivity. Just because someone has a six-foot-long to-do list doesn’t mean they are closer to achieving their goals. If the items on their list don’t focus on their priorities, they might be setting themselves up for disappointment.

Stop glorifying busyness. Try changing your mindset amid the 21st century’s craze for productivity. Start focusing on your priorities rather than trying to get more done. Once you get comfortable rejecting the pressure to do more, you will discover you can finally live a guilt-free, abundant life. Your ideal life will become your reality.

Try this

Clearly identify your priorities. Whenever you turn in circles and wonder if you should treat a task as essential, pause and breathe deeply, then ask yourself: Does it align with my main goal or North Star? Is it important? Is it beneficial? Is it realistic?

If you answer “yes” to these questions, raise a finger. If you counted three or more fingers, prioritize this task. If you raise two or fewer fingers, the task is less important and trivial.

Read it till the end?
